sentences.text: Oh, you have to shut the radio--
Most of the times, in Hungarian, they called Re-gee-na, or Re-geh-na,
My father and my mother always called me Rifkele.
That's the way I was called all the--
Well, it's one's Jewish or Hebrew name.
Well, this May I will be 68 years old.
I was born in Kisvarda, Hungary, and I can spell it.
I think we had 20,000 people living there.
I think they used to call her Pap Terez, because she was the daughter of a chief rabbi, and pap means rabbi, chief rabbi.
Well, the Hungarian name is Menyhert, which is a little difficult, but I'll spell--
But they also used to call him in his Jewish name, which is Mendel, M-E-N-D-E-L.
Menyhert-- do you want me to spell it?
Well, my father has been in America in the '30s.
He was a big wine wholesaler.
And he was going to sell some wine, even to Macy's.
But then he didn't like America.
He said, I don't want to bring up my children here.
And he came back in about two weeks.
And he had a businessman's visa.
And when it came to be 1938, '39, my mother begged him.
In fact, she used a very strong word.
That means save, but very strongly.
And my father refused up until the end of 1939, when it was very difficult to get out of the country.
What he did-- and he arrived here January, in this country, about 19-- 1940.
And there was no way of bringing us out here.
Because Hungary-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --went to war with America--
He was still hoping-- he was hoping and going to the government and working on it, but it was too late.
He didn't listen to my mother.
Because I guess he felt that his children will get away from religion.
I think that was if we come to this country.
And he just postponed it, and it was too late, unfortunately, when he came here.
Because my brother started to get away from it, you know.
And my household was a very intellectual because of my mother being so intelligent.
And I think that what he was afraid of.
The oldest one was called Yolan.
Cipi, ?] which was an endearing name, you know.
And my little sister, Hannah, Helen, [?
Hannaleh, ?] you know, we were all--
Oh, that's very difficult for me.
She couldn't have been more than 24--
Well, it just so happens that almost all the children were born two years apart.
She was five years younger than I was.
My mother and youngest little sister were gassed the night we arrived in Auschwitz, which was May 31, 1944.
Cipi, ?] was dragged to Belsen-- Bergen-Belsen.
And we heard, from other people, that she died of typhoid when the war ended already.
Until the very bitter end, you know.
There are four of us alive.
Well, I live in Great Neck.
Izabel lives in Manhattan, on the west side.
Chicha, ?] lives in Binghamton, New York.
And my brother, Phillip, lives in Montreal, Canada.
Well, there were a lot of poor people, poor Jews.
And they lived on this Jewish street called Csillag Utca.
It didn't mean Jewish star, just star.
And there were a number of-- there was even a banker.
There were a number of Jewish people who did very well.
And my father and mother were very well thought of because my mother was an exceptionally intelligent woman.
And my father was terribly bright.
So we were a family with what you would consider [NON-ENGLISH].
More like upper class, or you know, we just--
And I think we lived a nice life.
I was a little girl, and I had loads of friends.
And I went to a very good, private school.
And I thought, there's no place like Kisvarda--
--and I never want to leave it.
Whereas, my older sisters, as they got older, they all wanted to go to Budapest.
And everybody in the world, but I-- just give me Kisvarda.
I was very well known in my little town.
Because I think that they brought them all in the Kisvarda ghetto.
So I think that maybe Kisvarda and vicinity--
Well, that was his main occupation.
He would buy up vineyards before they even picked the grapes.
We had-- under our house we had a giant basement with giant vats of wine, you know?
And the-- the smaller dealers would come from the country and buy wine from my father.
You mean even towns that were smaller?
The towns were very much smaller.
Because they all had a bar.
No, this was for the Hungarians.
And later on, my father always ex-- exported to Switzerland, I think Holland, and maybe Denmark, wine.
If the wine came in successfully--
And if not, well, we had a little economic problem, like every business has its ups and downs.
Well, we were a large family.
And my mother always would have two poor little boys who went to school in my town.
So there were eight of us, and two more people.
Come for-- other towns, studying in Kisvarda.
And they needed to go somewhere on the Shabbat.
And my mother seeked them out.
And so we always had at least 10 people at the table.
And it was a beautiful dining room with this giant table and a white cloth, the damask cloth.
Or sometimes, we even had a tablecloth that my mother designed.
And with her, all futuristic designs--
And we had 32 candles burning every Shabbat.
Because we came from a very big family from my mother's side, you know.
I think that all those who died.
But it was beautiful, all those lights, candelabras, and then smaller ones.
And always a couple of maids.
And I used to help in the kitchen.
And I loved to be around my mother, and the maids.
And yes, there was fresh, homemade challah every single Saturday.
Yes, my father and my brother.
The girls didn't take such role in this whole Judaism.
We were kind of in the background.
No, but they knew how to read the prayer books.
I, when it came to me, I didn't even know how to read the prayer books.
Well, because in the school there would be one--
There would be a Jewish teacher coming and teaching the children read Hebrew, and some history, I'm sure.
The Jewish teacher came to the school.
When the Catholic children went to their priest--
You know, or the priest came, where the Jewish children had not a rabbi, but a teacher--
Well, I wasn't very much interested.
I was a very good student, very busy with my homework.
And I just got away with it.
It wasn't, somehow, in my interest.
Well, I was a very, very funny child.
I-- I used to-- anytime guests came, I had to entertain the guests.
They put me on top of the table, and I had to entertain.
And little by little, the whole city, Kisvarda, knew about me as somebody who's always appearing in all the school plays.
I once had five roles in the school plays.
It was so much that they changed my name for the fifth one-- [LAUGHTER] --because it was, you know, Regina Katz was in everything.
And I used to wear this Hungarian uniform for these occasions, which is very beautiful.
Oh well, it's like a peasant dress, white with a lot of ribbons on it, and a little vest from velvet.
I guess everybody thought that I'm just going to be a little actress.
I mean, this was nothing I could do about, you know?
I think that was in the beginning, in the-- in-- while we were in Hungary, yes.
No, I did go once to Budapest, and recited a poem there.
And it-- it looked like I may become a professional.
Actually, at the age-- oh, I went to nursery school.
And I even remember a little coat I had, a blue coat with white fur around it.
And I once got lost in nursery school.
And they brought me home, put me on the table.
But after nursery school, I-- at age six or close to it, I got into-- just elementary school.
And my oldest sisters preceded me in the same school.
And we were known as the Katz sisters.
And they all gotta be A's, you know?
There was no question about it.
The school was waiting for us in a way, you know?
So I had a standing in the school, you know?
So it felt good going to school every day?
Oh, I loved going to school.
Not so much in the schools.
But once you got out of the school, there were these hooligans, young boys, who would call you dirty Jew or all kinds of things.
Oh, this is before the Germans came in.
Because the Hungarians themselves have a history of being antisemitic, maybe for 1,000 years.
I-- I had some comfort because I was a good student, and I had a station, let's say, in life.
Well, my father didn't let him go to public school.
Didn't let-- didn't like the Jewish school, my father, for some reason.
He was a individual, and he believed in what he believed in.
And my oldest-- my-- one of my sisters, [?
--has taught her at home-- taught him at home.
Oh, well, then when he was old-- yes, he went to cheder, reluctantly.
And occasionally, he was on top, the best student.
And then he would leave it and become more modern.
Well, we didn't call it Hasidic, because it was like one grade away from it.
But I don't know what they would call it, but--
Yeah, but he pushed it up.
My father pushed it up too.
And my father didn't wear a shtreimel.
A shtreimel is the most Orthodox, which is very similar to what the Russian Jews wore, whom we never knew.
It's a velvet cap with, I think-- I think fur, which was a very good fur.
Mink all around it, but my father didn't believe in that.
A beaver hat is very much like a very fine-- and hat made out of, I guess, beaver or something, but it's not-- it wasn't furry.
And he-- it was-- kind of looked good on him.
But it wasn't the kind of hat that the Hungarian--
He didn't look like a real Hassid.
He had a beard, and he had his payots pulled up.
Yeah, something like that, with an umbrella, you know.
Yeah but he wore a jacket, which was just not that long.
And he would have an overcoat, a black overcoat, you know?
Yes, but I don't think they showed that.
If I remember, that it was always hidden.
Because you know, he was on the trains a lot.
Maybe he was just afraid to show it--
Because he went on this roller coaster of being a good son and then not wanting religion.
So there were problems in my family.
No, but I know that he went to-- he went to-- because my parents used to travel to Switzerland, because my father sold the Swiss wine.
And they took Phillip and they put him into a yeshiva in Montreux, Switzerland, which wasn't Hasidic, but it was religious.
And then, they put him even a fancier one in Hungary called Pap, in a town called Pap, P-A-P.
And he did very well for a while.
I don't know, but it was very famous and very good.
Well, it was-- my mother read every book.
We had Freud's when it came out.
We had poetry in the house.
We had poetry readings in the afternoon.
And my mother had friends who just came to talk to him-- to her about politics.
Because she was very, very bright--
Well, they were there for generations in Hajdunanas.
And then her brothers became-- and a very well-known family.
Well, I suppose so, but he was dead when I was born.
But my mother always said that his-- that her father was also a beautiful human being.
And he couldn't take the First World War, and he died of heart failure.
Well, I think they eventually they had the Nuremberg laws, which took away-- we had land also, from my grandparents on my father's side.
I couldn't go on to gymnasium.
And it was-- I think that it was-- everybody was affected by it.
